answer:
'Ishq' in Urdu poetic tradition, particularly in Sufi-influenced works like those of Allama Iqbal, often denotes an intense, all-consuming spiritual passion toward the Divine—entailing pain (*dard*), self-sacrifice, annihilation of the ego (*fana*), and ecstatic torment—far beyond the English "love"'s typical connotations of mutual romance, affection, or emotional attachment. "Love" fails when it domesticates this into something sentimental or interpersonal, obscuring the radical, existential stakes.[^1]

Here are three couplets from Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara Part 1* where a strictly romantic reading of the translated "love" would mask *ishq*'s mystical ferocity:

1. **Tere ishq ki inteha chahta hoon**  
   *Transliteration:* Tere ishq ki inteha chahta hoon / Meri saadgi dekh kya chahta hoon[^2]  
   *Translation:* The final reach of Your Love is what I seek; / Behold my simplicity, what I beseech is what I seek.[^3]  
   A romantic "love" suggests yearning for a beloved's embrace; here, *ishq* is the seeker's audacious pursuit of total union with God ("Your"), even at the cost of annihilation.

2. **Lutf kalaam kya jo na ho dil mein dard-e-ishq**  
   *Transliteration:* Lutf kalaam kya jo na ho dil mein dard-e-ishq / Bismil nahin hai to tu tadapna bhi chhor de[^4]  
   *Translation:* What joy is there in speech without the agony of passion's fire? / If you're not the sacrifice, this writhing in desire—abandon it.[^5]  
   English "love" evokes sweet eloquence; *ishq* demands heart-rending *dard* (pain/agony) and self-sacrifice (*bismil*, like a slaughtered goat), rendering mere words worthless without visceral torment.

3. **Ae Dard-e-ishq! hai gohar-e-aab-daar tu**  
   *Transliteration:* Ae Dard-e-ishq! hai gohar-e-aab-daar tu / Na-mehramon mein dekh na ho aashkaar tu[^6]  
   *Translation:* O, Anguish of Love! A pearl of purest water, you; / Be not revealed to the unworthy view.[^7]  
   "Love" sounds precious but gentle; *ishq* *is* the anguish (*dard*), a radiant yet hidden jewel unfit for profane eyes, personified as a secretive spiritual essence.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 17 ("Dard-e-Ishq"), [translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 12 ("Tere ishq ki inteha chahta hoon"), [transliteration](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/12-tere-ishq-ki-inteha-chahta-hoon/transliteration/#p-f7c4a3).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 12 ("Tere ishq ki inteha chahta hoon"), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/12-tere-ishq-ki-inteha-chahta-hoon/translation/#p-34168f).

[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 9 ("Majnoon ne sheher chhora to sehra bhi chhor de"), [transliteration](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/09-majnoon-ne-sheher-chhora-to-sehra-bhi-chhor-de/transliteration/#p-a0656b).

[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 9 ("Majnoon ne sheher chhora to sehra bhi chhor de"), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/09-majnoon-ne-sheher-chhora-to-sehra-bhi-chhor-de/translation/#p-fdec68).

[^6]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 17 ("Dard-e-Ishq"), [transliteration](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/transliteration/#p-787d3f).

[^7]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 17 ("Dard-e-Ishq"), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-6d19d8).
